brands.name: Holiday Inn Express
brands.notes: IHG's largest and fastest-growing brand with 2,800+ US hotels. A hotel-scale franchise: ground-up development runs roughly $13M-$19M, well beyond typical small-business franchise capital, and most economics are driven by real estate the franchisee must separately acquire/finance.

riskFlags.title: Hotel-scale capital requirement
riskFlags.detail: Total investment for a typical 93-room Holiday Inn Express runs $12.8M-$19.5M (Entrepreneur), and roughly $7.9M-$11.1M excluding land/real-estate acquisition per IHG's FDD framing. This is a developer/investor-class commitment, not an operator-level franchise.

riskFlags.title: Brand standards / PIP renovation obligations
riskFlags.detail: IHG-branded hotels are subject to periodic Property Improvement Plan (PIP) and brand-standard refresh requirements that can require substantial capital reinvestment over the franchise term, common across major flag-hotel systems.

nationwide.note: Franchising opportunities listed across United States regions; brand operates 2,800+ US locations.

totalInvestmentMin.note: Typical 93-room hotel, including real estate. IHG FDD also cites ~$7.9M-$11.1M excluding land acquisition.

franchiseFee.note: CONFLICT: VettedBiz cites a $50,000 application fee (reducible to $25K/$15K in some cases); Entrepreneur lists $75,000. Both refer to the initial/application fee.

royaltyPercent.note: 6% monthly royalty on gross rooms revenue; additional brand-marketing, technology and reservation fees apply.

totalUnits.note: Global Holiday Inn Express system; 2,400+ in the US per IHG. Other sources cite ~2,847.

yearFounded.note: Holiday Inn Express brand launched ~1991 (parent Holiday Inn dates to 1952). Entrepreneur lists 1952/1954 for the combined Holiday Inn lineage.

absenteeOwnership.note: Hotels typically run by on-site management/operators; ownership commonly investor/absentee with professional management.

isGrowing.note: IHG describes Holiday Inn Express as its largest and fastest-growing brand.

brands.name: RE/MAX
brands.notes: Real estate brokerage franchise founded 1973, franchising since 1975, with ~140K+ agents globally. Faces structural headwinds: US agent-count declines and a $55M antitrust commission-lawsuit settlement that reshaped industry commission practices.

riskFlags.title: $55M buyer-broker commission antitrust settlement
riskFlags.detail: RE/MAX agreed in September 2023 to pay $55 million to settle the Sitzer/Burnett, Moehrl and Nosalek class actions alleging Sherman Antitrust violations over inflated seller-paid commissions. The settlements (alongside NAR's) materially changed how US real estate commissions are structured.

riskFlags.title: Declining US agent count
riskFlags.detail: US agent count fell 6.0% YoY to 56,494 by Q3 2023; in Q1 2024 total agent count declined again (-0.2%) with US+Canada combined down 4.3% YoY as the housing market slowed. Agent count is the core revenue driver for RE/MAX franchisees.

nationwide.note: RE/MAX franchises offices across all US states and internationally; nationwide franchise availability.

totalInvestmentMin.note: Range varies by office density; sources cite ~$40K-$245K.

franchiseFee.note: Low end (low-density, lump-sum) $17,500; high-density/financed up to $37,500.

totalUnits.note: Figure represents US franchised offices per VettedBiz; RE/MAX operates ~8,000 offices and 140K+ agents globally. Office-count metrics vary widely by source/scope.

ownerTypes.note: Broker-owner model; many owners are licensed brokers.

brands.name: Express Employment Professionals
brands.notes: Staffing/recruiting franchise founded 1983, franchising since 1985, with 840+ offices. Strong Item 19: mature offices average well over $5M in annual sales; ranked #1 in staffing/recruiting on Entrepreneur's 2026 list.

riskFlags.title: High revenue but margin-driven model
riskFlags.detail: Item 19 sales figures are gross staffing billings, not profit; the royalty is structured as a share of gross margin (Express retains 40% of temp/contract gross margin and 10% of direct-hire receipts), so franchisee net economics depend heavily on margin and labor costs rather than top-line revenue.

nationwide.note: Lists franchising opportunities across all 50 states plus DC; seeking franchisees nationwide.

totalInvestmentMin.note: CONFLICT: Franchise Chatter cites $91,700-$213,000; ranges vary by territory size.

franchiseFee.note: Up to $40,000; varies by market/territory size (as low as $20,000).

royaltyPercent.note: Entrepreneur lists 8% royalty + 2% ad. FDD describes it as a gross-margin share (Express retains 40% of temp gross margin / 10% of direct-hire receipts).

avgUnitRevenue.note: Item 19 (2024 FDD): median annual gross revenue ~$4.4M; offices open 2+ years average ~$5.3M. Gross staffing billings, not profit.

totalUnits.note: US franchise units; 860+ globally across US, Canada, South Africa, Australia, New Zealand.

isGrowing.note: Ranked #1 in Staffing/Recruiting on Entrepreneur's 2026 Franchise 500.

brands.name: Jackson Hewitt Tax Service
brands.notes: #2 US retail tax-prep franchise founded 1982, franchising since 1986, with thousands of locations including Walmart kiosks. Low entry cost but high 15% royalty + 6% ad fee, and the brand carries a history of fraud litigation and a 2024 no-poach antitrust settlement.

riskFlags.title: $10.8M no-poach antitrust settlement (2024)
riskFlags.detail: Jackson Hewitt agreed to pay over $10 million to settle litigation alleging it required franchisees to sign no-poach agreements (from at least 2003) that restricted recruiting/hiring of tax preparers across franchises, suppressing wages.

riskFlags.title: Historic franchisee fraud litigation (2007 DOJ)
riskFlags.detail: In 2007 the US Department of Justice sued five corporations operating 125+ Jackson Hewitt franchise stores and 24 individuals, alleging pervasive fraud (false returns, phony W-2s) with more than $70M in alleged Treasury losses. The episode contributed to a sharp market-share decline.

riskFlags.title: Structural decline of retail tax-prep
riskFlags.detail: Retail tax preparation has lost share since the recession as customers shift to lower-cost online filing; Jackson Hewitt reportedly lost nearly 50% of its market share over a four-year stretch, declining faster than competitors.

riskFlags.title: High ongoing fee load
riskFlags.detail: Royalty of 15% plus a 6.5% advertising fee is high relative to most service franchises, compressing franchisee margins on a seasonal revenue stream.

nationwide.note: Operates franchised and company-owned locations nationwide, including kiosks in ~3,000 Walmart stores across the US.

totalInvestmentMin.note: Existing-franchisee standard business ~$49,350-$80,500; new franchisee standard business ~$96,050-$127,500.

adFundPercent.note: Cited as 6% to 6.5% advertising fee across sources.

totalUnits.note: ~5,167 US locations (2,744 franchised, 2,423 company/kiosk) per one source; brand cites ~6,000 total. Counts vary widely by season and source.

canRunPartTime.note: Seasonal tax business; many operators run part-time/seasonally.

brands.name: FASTSIGNS
brands.notes: Sign and visual-communications franchise founded 1985, franchising since 1986, with 650+ US centers. Strong Item 19 (avg gross sales ~$1.11M per center, 2025 FDD), SBA-backed financing program, and a 50% veteran/first-responder franchise-fee discount.

riskFlags.title: B2B sales-intensive, multi-staff model
riskFlags.detail: FASTSIGNS centers are full retail/production locations (~$231K-$386K to open) requiring B2B sales effort and staff; gross sales (~$1.11M AUV) are not profit, and top-quartile discretionary profit is cited at ~32.5% in Item 19, implying meaningful spread between strong and weak units.

nationwide.note: Franchising across the USA (and Canada); 650+ US centers, 775+ worldwide.

royaltyPercent.note: Service fee 3% of gross sales year 1, then 6% from year 2 onward.

adFundPercent.note: 1% of gross sales year 1, then 2% from year 2 onward.

avgUnitRevenue.note: Item 19 (2025 FDD): average gross sales $1,111,091 across 684 centers. Gross sales, not profit; top quartile ~32.5% discretionary profit.

sbaApproved.note: FASTSIGNS offers an SBA-backed financing program for approved franchisees.

veteranDiscount.note: 50% off initial franchise fee ($24,875) for veterans and first responders.

totalUnits.note: 650+ US centers; 745 worldwide. FASTSIGNS site cites 775+ worldwide.
